agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A production logistics system enforces a 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier invariant with supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided across multiple carriers without supervisor approval, directly contradicting two established domain invariants.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, currently guaranteed to have exactly one assigned Carrier at dispatch time."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The transport entity responsible for delivering a Shipment. Bound 1:1 to a Shipment once assigned."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The act of binding a Carrier to a Shipment. Once locked, only a Supervisor can change it. This is a domain invariant."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the sole authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a locked Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A proposed new concept — a child Shipment derived from splitting a parent Shipment. Does NOT exist in the current domain model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"The proposed automatic decomposition of an oversized Shipment into multiple Sub-Shipments, each assigned to a different Carrier."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment is released for transport. The system guarantees a valid Carrier assignment at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a data consistency and state integrity constraint. A Shipment without exactly one Carrier at dispatch is undeliverable — the system is in a corrupt state. Accountability, insurance, chain-of-custody, and billing all depend on this 1:1 binding.","testable_assertion":"At any point after Carrier assignment and before delivery completion, querying a Shipment returns exactly one Carrier ID, never zero, never more than one."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Unsupervised reassignment breaks chain-of-custody, creates liability gaps, and opens the door to fraud or routing errors. The Supervisor gate exists to enforce accountability.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to change the Carrier on a locked Shipment without a Supervisor credential must be rejected by the system."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"The auto-split feature proposes that shipments exceeding 500 kg are automatically divided into multiple sub-shipments, each with a different carrier, without supervisor involvement.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The 500 kg threshold is a business policy — a VP could change it to 600 kg tomorrow. The bypass of supervisor approval is a policy relaxation, not a physical constraint. However, this rule as stated CONTRADICTS BR-1 and BR-2.","testable_assertion":"N/A — this rule cannot be implemented as stated without violating existing invariants."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment weighs exactly 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — the spec says 'exceeds 500 kg', so exactly 500 kg should NOT trigger auto-split, but this boundary needs explicit confirmation."},{"scenario":"A shipment is 501 kg but contains a single indivisible item (e.g., one 501 kg machine)","expected_behavior":"Auto-split is physically impossible. The feature has no concept of item-level splittability. System must handle unsplittable oversized shipments gracefully."},{"scenario":"Auto-split creates a sub-shipment that itself exceeds 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Recursive splitting? The spec is silent. Risk of infinite loops or nonsensical decomposition."},{"scenario":"Carrier is already assigned and locked, then the shipment weight is updated to exceed 500 kg","expected_behavior":"Does auto-split trigger post-assignment? This would require reassignment, which demands Supervisor approval under BR-2 — direct conflict."},{"scenario":"A split shipment has one sub-shipment delivered and another lost","expected_behavior":"Liability, insurance claims, and partial delivery handling are undefined. Which Carrier is accountable for the parent shipment? The 1:1 invariant currently makes this unambiguous."},{"scenario":"Billing and invoicing for split shipments","expected_behavior":"Current billing assumes one Carrier per Shipment. Multi-carrier billing, cost allocation, and dispute resolution are entirely undefined."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Significant risk. The 1:1 Carrier-Shipment binding underpins invoicing, insurance claims, and liability assignment. Breaking it without a clear financial model for multi-carrier cost allocation could create billing disputes, unrecoverable costs on partial deliveries, and insurance gaps.","operational":"High complexity increase. Every downstream system — tracking, dispatch, proof-of-delivery, SLA monitoring — assumes one Carrier per Shipment. Auto-split introduces a parent-child Shipment hierarchy that ripples through the entire operational stack. Maintenance burden multiplies.","user":"Customers currently track one shipment with one carrier. Splitting introduces confusion: multiple tracking numbers, multiple delivery windows, partial delivery scenarios. Without careful UX, customer satisfaction will drop.","risk":"CRITICAL. Two production invariants would be violated simultaneously. This is not a feature addition — it is a domain model restructuring. Shipping it as described risks: (1) data corruption where shipments have ambiguous carrier ownership, (2) liability gaps where no single carrier is accountable, (3) supervisor bypass creating an unaudited reassignment path that could be exploited.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle parent-child shipment hierarchies, coordinate multiple carriers per logical shipment, and manage partial delivery scenarios that don't exist today."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their gating authority is bypassed for auto-split scenarios, reducing oversight and accountability on the highest-risk shipments (the heaviest ones)."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Must accept sub-shipments derived from a parent they didn't originally contract for. SLA terms, liability, and compensation models are undefined."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Receive multiple deliveries for a single order. Partial delivery risk. Multiple tracking numbers to monitor."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Cost allocation across multiple carriers for a single logical shipment is undefined. Dispute resolution becomes multi-party."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody is broken. Insurance policies likely assume 1:1 carrier-shipment. Regulatory reporting may require restructuring."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction with two established production invariants (1:1 carrier assignment and supervisor-gated reassignment). Until the product team resolves whether this is a domain model restructuring or a misunderstanding of the existing model, no acceptance criteria can be confidently defined. The 500 kg threshold itself is a soft policy, but the structural changes required are fundamental.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and the existing 1:1 Carrier-Shipment invariant is unambiguous","The contradiction between bypassing Supervisor approval and BR-2 is unambiguous","The ripple effects on billing, liability, and tracking are real and significant"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to restructure the domain model or is unaware of the invariants they're breaking","How item-level splittability should work","Whether 'sub-shipment' is a new aggregate or a child of the existing Shipment aggregate","The correct resolution path — several valid approaches exist"],"assumptions":["The existing invariants (BR-1, BR-2) are correctly stated and currently enforced in production","The product team has not already approved a domain model restructuring that would relax these invariants","The 500 kg threshold is a business policy, not a regulatory requirement"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature directly violates BR-1 (one Shipment = one Carrier). If a shipment is split across multiple carriers, either the invariant must be relaxed or 'sub-shipment' must be modeled as a first-class aggregate that independently satisfies BR-1. The feature as stated is incompatible with the current domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Carriers","Customers","Finance","Legal"],"options":["A: Introduce a 'Sub-Shipment' aggregate. Each Sub-Shipment is a full Shipment that independently satisfies BR-1 (exactly one Carrier). The parent becomes a 'Shipment Group' or 'Order' — a coordination concept, not a dispatchable unit. BR-1 is preserved at the Sub-Shipment level.","B: Relax BR-1 to allow many-to-many Carrier-Shipment relationships. This is a fundamental domain model change with massive downstream impact.","C: Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model. Offer an alternative: flag oversized shipments for Supervisor-mediated manual splitting, preserving both invariants."],"recommendation":"Option A. It preserves BR-1 at the sub-shipment level, introduces a clean domain concept (Shipment Group), and avoids relaxing a production invariant. However, it requires significant modeling work and stakeholder alignment before implementation begins."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature bypasses Supervisor approval for what is effectively a carrier reassignment operation. The heaviest shipments — arguably the highest-risk — would be the ones with the LEAST oversight. This inverts the risk model.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Legal","Compliance"],"options":["A: Require Supervisor approval for auto-split (contradicts the 'without requiring supervisor involvement' requirement but preserves BR-2).","B: Create a new authorization model where auto-split is a system-initiated action with its own audit trail, distinct from manual reassignment. Supervisor is notified but not gated.","C: Allow auto-split without supervisor approval but add a post-hoc review step where supervisors can intervene within a time window before dispatch."],"recommendation":"Option B. System-initiated splits with full audit trails and supervisor notification preserves the spirit of BR-2 (accountability and oversight) while enabling automation. The key is that the system — not an individual actor — is making the decision, which is a different trust model than an unsupervised human reassignment."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one shipment, breaking the 1:1 Carrier-Shipment invariant.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier."},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses Supervisor approval for carrier assignment changes on the highest-risk (heaviest) shipments.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Once a Carrier is assigned, only a Supervisor can reassign."}]}

The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would allow shipments over 500 kg to be divided across multiple carriers without supervisor approval, directly contradicting two established domain invariants.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods
